questions.question: What three findings make up Cushing's Triad?
options: A. Hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnea
B. Hypertension, bradycardia, and an altered respiratory pattern
C. Fever, rash, and vomiting
D. Hypoglycemia, diaphoresis, tachycardia
questions.rationale: Cushing's triad — hypertension, bradycardia, and altered respirations — signals significantly increased intracranial pressure.

questions.question: What does the FAST stroke mnemonic assess?
options: A. Fever, Airway, Speech, Temperature
B. Face, Arm, Speech, Time
C. Fatigue, Anxiety, Sweating, Tremor
D. Family history, Age, Symptoms, Treatment
questions.rationale: FAST stands for Face, Arm, Speech, Time — a rapid field stroke screening tool.

questions.question: Why is establishing the 'Time' (last known well) so critical in a stroke assessment?
options: A. It has no clinical importance
B. Eligibility for time-sensitive stroke treatments depends on precisely knowing when the patient was last normal
C. It only matters for documentation purposes
D. Time is only relevant for cardiac arrest calls
questions.rationale: Time-sensitive stroke treatments have windows defined from last known well, not from symptom discovery, making this the single most treatment-relevant data point in FAST.

questions.question: A patient presents with a headache. What should be performed to rule out serious causes?
options: A. No further assessment is needed
B. A neurological exam
C. An abdominal exam only
D. A cardiac 12-lead only
questions.rationale: A neurological exam in a headache patient helps distinguish a benign headache from stroke, hemorrhage, or infection presenting as head pain.

questions.question: Which triad of findings is classic for meningitis?
options: A. Fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status
B. Hypertension, bradycardia, altered respirations
C. JVD, muffled heart tones, hypotension
D. Wheeze, stridor, cyanosis
questions.rationale: Meningitis is associated with fever, nuchal rigidity (neck stiffness), and altered mental status.

questions.question: A patient presents with unilateral weakness and slurred speech. What must be checked immediately, before assuming stroke?
options: A. Temperature
B. Blood glucose
C. Pulse oximetry only
D. Weight
questions.rationale: Diabetic emergencies (hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia) can mimic stroke symptoms; glucose must be checked because hypoglycemia is rapidly reversible while true stroke is not.

questions.question: A patient with stroke-like symptoms has their glucose corrected and the deficit fully resolves within minutes. What does this suggest?
options: A. A true ischemic stroke
B. The symptoms were a hypoglycemic stroke mimic, not a true stroke
C. A hemorrhagic stroke
D. Nothing — strokes commonly resolve this quickly
questions.rationale: True strokes do not resolve with glucose administration; rapid full resolution after correcting glucose indicates the presentation was a hypoglycemic mimic.

questions.question: What does Cushing's Triad physiologically represent?
options: A. A normal physiologic response to exercise
B. The brainstem's response to compression from significantly increased intracranial pressure
C. A sign of dehydration
D. An allergic reaction
questions.rationale: Cushing's triad reflects brainstem compression from rising intracranial pressure — often due to bleeding, swelling, or a mass effect — and is a late, ominous sign.

questions.question: Which headache presentation should raise the MOST concern for a serious underlying cause?
options: A. Mild headache that improves with rest
B. 'Worst headache of my life' with new focal neurological deficits
C. A headache the patient has had many times before with the same character
D. A headache relieved by over-the-counter analgesics
questions.rationale: A sudden, severe ('worst of my life') headache with new focal deficits is a classic red flag for hemorrhage or another serious neurological emergency.

questions.question: A patient has fever, neck stiffness, and confusion. What should be strongly suspected?
options: A. Simple tension headache
B. Meningitis
C. Migraine
D. Dehydration alone
questions.rationale: Fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status together form the classic meningitis triad and should prompt suspicion of this infectious, potentially rapidly progressive condition.

questions.question: Why is Cushing's Triad considered a LATE sign rather than an early warning?
options: A. It appears at the very onset of any brain injury
B. By the time all three findings fully manifest, intracranial pressure elevation is already severe
C. It is unrelated to intracranial pressure
D. It only occurs in children
questions.rationale: Cushing's triad reflects an advanced physiologic response to significantly elevated ICP — its full appearance indicates the process is already severe, not just beginning.

questions.question: Which combination of vital sign changes should make you suspect rising intracranial pressure?
options: A. Hypotension and tachycardia
B. Hypertension, bradycardia, and an irregular respiratory pattern
C. Normal blood pressure and normal heart rate
D. Hypertension and tachycardia only
questions.rationale: This combination is Cushing's triad, a hallmark of significantly elevated intracranial pressure requiring urgent recognition and transport.

questions.question: In a suspected stroke call, what is the correct order of priority for the FIRST two actions?
options: A. Full head-to-toe exam, then glucose check
B. Check blood glucose and perform FAST assessment (including establishing last known well time)
C. Transport immediately with no assessment
D. Obtain a detailed medication list before any exam
questions.rationale: Checking glucose (to rule out a reversible mimic) and performing the FAST assessment with a precise last-known-well time are the priority early actions in a suspected stroke.

questions.question: Which statement correctly relates diabetic emergencies to stroke assessment?
options: A. Diabetic emergencies never affect neurological presentation
B. Diabetic emergencies can present with stroke-like symptoms, making glucose checks mandatory in suspected stroke
C. Only hyperglycemia can mimic stroke, never hypoglycemia
D. Glucose checks are optional in stroke assessment
questions.rationale: Both hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia can present with stroke-like symptoms (weakness, slurred speech, altered mentation), so glucose must always be checked in a suspected stroke.
